coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photoluminescence of layered terbium hydroxides by graphene quantum dots in-situ synthesized in the interlayer
coredata.dc:creator: Sang, Song
Shao, Baiyi
Zhang, Xiaobao
Guo, Aoping
Cui, Fangming
Yang, Xiaojing
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) intercalated layered terbium hydroxides (LTbH) were prepared through in-situ hydrothermal carbonization of the intercalated citrate anions (CA) and showed an enhanced photoluminescence (PL) due to the sensitization of GQDs for LTbHs. Structural and compositional analysis indicated the GQDs were consisted of well-identified hexagonal lattice of graphene, together with some defects caused by oxygen-containing functional groups. The basal spacings decreased from 8.6 Å of NO3ˉ-LTbHs to 7.3 Å of GQD-LTbHs resulted by the strong interaction between the intercalated GQDs and LTbHs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Layered terbium hydroxide
Hydrothermal carbonization
Sensitization
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD and (B) FTIR spectra: curve (a) NO3ˉ-, (b) CA- and (c) GQD intercalated LTbHs.
(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM image of GQDs, (C) dark field TEM and corresponding elemental mappings of (D) C and (E) Tb.
(A) Raman and (B) C1s XPS spectra of GQDs.
(A) PL excitation spectra monitored at 544 nm and (B) PL emission spectra excited by 352 nm laser: curve (a) NO3ˉ, (b) CA and (c) GQD intercalated LTbHs.
PL decay curves of (A) NO3ˉ-LTbH and (B) GQD-LTbH.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photoluminescence of layered terbium hydroxides by graphene quantum dots in-situ synthesized in the interlayer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Materials, College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, 19-Xinjiekouwai Street, Haidian District, Beijing, 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Materials, College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, 19-Xinjiekouwai Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100875, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology, China Academy of Space Technology, 104-Youyi Road, Haidian District, Beijing, 100094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology, China Academy of Space Technology, 104-Youyi Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100094, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) intercalated layered terbium hydroxides (LTbH) were prepared through in-situ hydrothermal carbonization of the intercalated citrate anions (CA) and showed an enhanced photoluminescence (PL) due to the sensitization of GQDs for LTbHs. Structural and compositional analysis indicated the GQDs were consisted of well-identified hexagonal lattice of graphene, together with some defects caused by oxygen-containing functional groups. The basal spacings decreased from 8.6 Å of NO3ˉ-LTbHs to 7.3 Å of GQD-LTbHs resulted by the strong interaction between the intercalated GQDs and LTbHs.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) intercalated layered terbium hydroxides (LTbH) were prepared through in-situ hydrothermal carbonization of the intercalated citrate anions and showed an enhanced photoluminescence due to the sensitization of GQDs for LTbHs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The layered rare earth hydroxides (LRHs) with the general formula [R2(OH)6-mAm]•nH2O (R is trivalent rare-earth ion and A represents intercalated anion, 0.5 ≤ m ≤ 2) usually presented characteristic photoluminescence (PL) by the rare earth ions, which could be further tuned by the intercalated sensitizer introduced into the interlayer by the anion exchange process [1–4]. The unique layer structure and PL feature made LRHs great application potentials in illumination, bioimaging and anion detection [5,6]. However, the PL efficiency of LRHs was relatively low because of the non-radiative energy consumption by hydroxyl group (-OH) and water molecules coordinated with rare earth ions and the f-f transition prohibition of rare-earth ions.

An efficient strategy to improve the PL efficiency was the sensitization of LRHs by the intercalated organic anions which could transfer the absorbed energy to the rare-earth ions [5–8]. And the intercalation of the organic anions could be easily realized by the anion exchange process from the precursor NO3ˉ-LRHs without destroying the layered structure of LRHs. Recently, single-layered GQDs were obtained in the confined interlayer of layered double hydroxides (LDH) by the hydrothermal carbonization of citrate anions (CA) intercalated LDHs [9]. Meanwhile, GQDs have shown unique PL properties due to their tunable/opened band gap by heteroatoms and functional groups and the quantum confinement effect of conjugated π electrons [10,11]. The broad PL emission of GQDs were supposed to match the excited states of rare earth ions [12], and the excellent absorption of GQDs made them to be ideal sensitizer for energy transfer to rare earth ions in LRHs [13,14].

Herein, we synthesized GQDs intercalated layered terbium hydroxides (LTbH) by hydrothermal carbonization of CA-LTbHs at 160 °C for 8 h, which showed enhanced PL intensity due to the sensitization of GQDs. The GQDs were in-situ synthesized in the interlayer of LTbHs from CA precursor that was introduced in the interlayer by ion exchange process.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD and FTIR spectra of NO3ˉ-LTbH, CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH. The XRD patterns of the samples corresponded to the monoclinic symmetry of the LRHs as previous reported [15], indicating type m = 1 in [R2(OH)6-mAm]•nH2O. The layered structure of these three samples could be observed by the SEM images in Fig. S1. The (002) diffractions of CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH moved to higher 2θ angles compared with that of NO3ˉ-LTbH (Fig. 1 A), indicating the basal spacings (d basal) of LTbH were reduced from 8.6 to 7.7 and 7.3 Å after the CA intercalation and GQD in-situ formation, which could be attributed to the CA/GQD lying in a horizontal orientation between the LTbH layers and strong interactions between carboxyl of the intercalated CA/GQD and positive LTbH layer [6]. And the (004) diffraction peak, as secondary diffractions of (002), shifted to large angle as well due to the decrease of d basal. The (220) diffractions of CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH retained the same 2θ angles as that of NO3ˉ-LTbH (Fig. 1 A), specifying the intra-layer planes of LTbH were remained, as well as the crystal symmetry of LTbH, after the CA exchange and GQD in-situ synthesis. The additional weak diffraction peak appears at 5.5 Å in CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH reveals the generation of a little Tb(OH)3 phase, which might be caused by local charge imbalance brought by the random intercalation of CA. That was consistent with the increase of –OH ratios in CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH compared with that of NO3ˉ-LTbH, shown in Table S1.

All the samples presented the same absorptions at wavenumbers of around 600 and 1610 cm−1, corresponding to Tb–O vibration of LTbHs and O–H vibration of the combined water (Fig. 1 B). NO3ˉ-LTbH showed the dramatical NO3ˉ absorption at 1350–1390 cm−1. In contrast, CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH showed additional relative weak absorptions at 1380 and 1520 cm−1, which corresponded to the symmetry and asymmetry vibrations of –COOˉ by the intercalated CA and GQD [6,9]. The removement of NO3ˉ in CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH could also be seen from the chemical analysis results of different LTbHs in measured by ICP and CHN analyzer (Table S1), and the Tb/C molar ratios calculated from composition analysis declares the content of C has no remarked change during CA transforming to GQD, the construction of the samples could be reflected by respectively calculated chemical formulae.

Fig. 3 shows the Raman and C1s XPS spectra of GQDs. The G band (1591 cm−1) representing sp2 carbon skeleton and the D band (1358 cm−1) representing defects/disorders in Raman spectrum (Fig. 3A) are observable. The Intensity (ID/IG) ratio of GQD in-situ synthesized between LTbH layers is 1.00, similar to pristine GQD as reported [17], indicating the defective graphene structure of the in-situ synthesized GQDs brought by heteroatoms doping [18]. [19] The C1s XPS spectra of GQDs could be fitted to four sub-peaks at binding energy of 284.6, 285.7, 286.4 and 288.0 eV belong to CC, C–C/C–H, C–OH and CO bonds [20], indicating the oxidized state of the GQDs and the relevant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the in-situ synthesized GQDs, which increased the interaction between the GQDs and the positive LTbH layers and was consistent with above TEM and XRD results.

The UV–vis spectra of three powder samples in Fig. S4 demonstrated the strongest absorption was detectable around 250 nm, and the GQD solution showed two absorption peak at ~227 nm and ~270 nm (Fig. S5), origin from π→π* transition of aromatic CC bond and n→π* transition of CO bond respectively, the “n band” was corresponding to the existence of oxygen functional groups [18]. [21,22] The PL properties of the samples were investigated by the PL emission and excitation spectra as shown in Fig. 4 . A dramatic PL shoulder at 250 nm in excitation spectra was presented by GQD-LTbH compared with those of NO3ˉ-LTbH and CA-LTbH (Fig. 4 A), which should originate from the π→π* by the intercalated GQDs [6]. The PL emission spectra of the samples were excited by 352 nm laser, where all samples showed strongest emissions (Fig. 4 B) under irradiation of near-UV light, to compare the PL intensity of different samples. And all samples showed the characteristic 5D4 → 7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4 and 3) emissions of Tb3+ at 490, 544, 587 and 622 nm in the PL emission spectra (Fig. 4 B) [23]. The PL emission spectrum of GQD-LTbH showed obviously higher intensity than those of NO3ˉ-LTbH and CA-LTbH. Besides, the strongest 5D4→7F5 emission peak of Tb3+ enhanced dramatically after the intercalation of GQDs with excitation wavelength monitored at 258 nm in deep-UV region, while the NO3ˉ-LTbH barely showed any fluorescence, as shown in Fig. S6. The chemical formulas calculated from the composition analysis results in Table S1 verified the –OH ratio of CA-LTbH and GQD-LTbH samples were very close and increased slightly compared with the nitrate type precursor, but the PL emission intensity of GQD-LTbH was enhanced other than decrease caused by the extra non-radiative energy consumption by –OH, all of the results above demonstrate the excellent UV light absorption ability of GQDs and effective energy transfer from the GQDs to Tb3+ ions, resulting in the sensitization effect, which also could be directly observed by the optical photos in Fig. S7.

In addition, the PL decay curves of LTbHs intercalate by GQDs and NO3ˉ were presented in Fig. 5 , fitted in line with the double exponential decay trend. The PL lifetime τ of GQD-LTbHs was 410.8 μs which was distinctly higher than the τ = 169.7 μs of NO3ˉ-LTbHs.

In summary, we synthesized GQD intercalated LTbHs by in-situ carbonization of CA that introduced into the interlayer by ion exchange. The GQDs showed well-identified hexagonal lattice of graphene and strong interaction with the positive LTbH layers indicated by the reduced basal spacing by GQD-LTbHs. A dramatic PL intensity enhancement of GQD-LTbHs was achieved by sensitization of the intercalated GQDs demonstrated by the PL characterizations.

Song Sang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Baiyi Shao: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaobao Zhang: Resources, Data curation. Aoping Guo: Validation, Investigation. Fangming Cui: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xiaojing Yang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was support by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 51572031) and the Innovative Program of Qian Xuesen Laboratory of Space Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2021.111424.
